---
title: "Backtesting a Strategy"
date: "2026-06-18"
categories: [algorithmic-trading]
description: "A quick intro to running backtests on algorithmic strategies."
image: thumbnail.png
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: '@*'
---

Backtesting is the process of simulating a trading strategy on historical data to evaluate its performance. Done carefully, it provides a useful signal about whether a strategy has merit — done carelessly, it produces misleading results.

## The Basic Setup

A backtest requires three components: a **signal** (when to trade), a **sizing rule** (how much to trade), and a **cost model** (what execution costs). Getting all three right matters — an impressive backtest with no transaction costs tells you very little.

## Common Pitfalls

The most dangerous backtesting mistakes are look-ahead bias (using future information that wouldn't have been available at the time) and overfitting (tuning parameters until the in-sample performance looks good, at the expense of out-of-sample validity).

## Evaluation Metrics

Raw return is a poor measure of strategy quality. Better metrics include the Sharpe ratio, maximum drawdown, and the Calmar ratio — each capturing a different dimension of risk-adjusted performance.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<hr class="revision-divider">
<details>
<summary>Revision History</summary>

- June 2026 — First published

</details>
